coredata.dc:title: S, N co-doped graphene quantum dots fabricated by rapid microwave-assisted pyrolysis and their optical properties
coredata.dc:creator: Vu Tuyet Nhung, Trinh
Van Tuyen, Ho
Tran, Ngo
Xuan Hung, Le
coredata.dc:description: Recently, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have attracted the interest of scientists as a potential material for applications in antibacterial agents. Besides, low-cost synthesis, environmentally friendly, safe in use, easy-to-perform technique is one of the urgent requirements to apply this material. This research presents the synthesis of sulfur (S) and nitrogen (N) co-doped GQDs (S,N-GQDs) by microwave-assisted pyrolysis, which is a rapid, simple, and green method, as well as improved their dispersion in water for antibacterial purposes. The particle size of fabricated S,N-GQDs was 3 nm. XRD patterns reveal that S,N-GQDs have a crystalline structure with a carbogenic core. Both the Sπ* → Cπ and Nπ* → Cπ transitions contribute to the luminescence of the S,N-GQDs material, whose local luminescence peaks at 448 nm, this peak changed with various pH values. High quantum yield depends on both the power of microwave and pH values.
coredata.dcterms:subject: GQDs
Doped-GQDs
S,N-GQDs, absorption
Photoluminescence spectra
Quantum efficiency
Microwave-assisted pyrolysis

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research is funded by the Vietnam National Foundation for Science and Technology Development (NAFOSTED) under grant number 103.03-2020.22 .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) XRD patterns of GQDs and S,N-GQDs, (b) Raman scattering spectra of GQDs and S,N-GQDs, (c) FT-IR spectra of GQDs and S,N-GQDs and (d) TEM images of S,N-GQDs,.
PL spectra of GQDs fabricated with different heteroatoms. Inset: Absorption spectra of the samples with different heteroatoms.
PL spectra as a function of microwave power for S,N-GQDs samples fabricated by microwave-assisted pyrolysis. Inset: absorption spectra of S,N-GQDs samples with different microwave powers.
PL spectra as a function of pH values of S,N-GQDs samples fabricated by microwave-assisted pyrolysis. Inset: absorption spectra of S,N-GQDs samples with the varying pH values.
Diagram of possible energy levels and their absorption and emission mechanisms.
QY values as a function of microwave power of S,N-GQDs samples fabricated by microwave-assisted pyrolysis.

head.ce:title: S, N co-doped graphene quantum dots fabricated by rapid microwave-assisted pyrolysis and their optical properties

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Medicine, College of Medicine and Pharmacy, Duy Tan University, DaNang 550000, Viet Nam

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Medicine, College of Medicine and Pharmacy, Duy Tan University

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Research and Development, Duy Tan University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Recently, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have attracted the interest of scientists as a potential material for applications in antibacterial agents. Besides, low-cost synthesis, environmentally friendly, safe in use, easy-to-perform technique is one of the urgent requirements to apply this material. This research presents the synthesis of sulfur (S) and nitrogen (N) co-doped GQDs (S,N-GQDs) by microwave-assisted pyrolysis, which is a rapid, simple, and green method, as well as improved their dispersion in water for antibacterial purposes. The particle size of fabricated S,N-GQDs was 3 nm. XRD patterns reveal that S,N-GQDs have a crystalline structure with a carbogenic core. Both the Sπ* → Cπ and Nπ* → Cπ transitions contribute to the luminescence of the S,N-GQDs material, whose local luminescence peaks at 448 nm, this peak changed with various pH values. High quantum yield depends on both the power of microwave and pH values.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) with nanostructures have received a lot of interest from scientists in recent years because of their applications in light-emitting diodes (LED), solar cells, bioimaging, electronic displays [1–3]. The luminescence properties of GQDs greatly depend on the quantum confinement effect and GQDs’s size [2–5]. When GQDs are in the form of non-dimensional graphite with a diameter of less than 10 nm, they will have many special properties. At this nanoscale, when the dimensionality satisfies the Broglie wavelength of the carriers, a quantum confinement effect occurs, leading to a restriction of the carrier's motion and a change in the magnetic energy spectrum from continuous to intermittent [5]. In addition, the surface states become more mobile, causing an expansion of the band gap. Moreover, the doping of heteroatoms and the surface functionalization of GQDs provide many opportunities for practical applications. GQDs not only possess special properties derived from two-dimensional (2D) graphene but also exhibit attractive physicochemical properties of QDs, including boundary effects, confinement effects, non-zero band gaps… Therefore, they have potential applications in the energy, electronics, optical industries [2] and in the fields of fluorescent lamps [3,5,6] and photovoltaic devices [7,8]. Besides that, biomedical applications, including antibacterial agents [9–12], biomedical labels [5,13], etc., have been the main fields of application of GQDs in recent years.

As can be mentioned above, the doping of heteroatoms into GQDs leads to the change in their physicochemical properties and this makes the diversity and abundance of GQDs’s applications. The heteroatoms, including N, S, B, Cl, F, etc., are commonly used to dope into GQDs for tuning the optical properties of GQDs [14]. Among them, N and S are the most used elements because they have similar sizes, valence electron numbers and electronegativity as carbon (C) [4]. These S,N co-doped GQDs are usually synthesized by pyrolysis [15,16] or hydrothermal method [17,18] from citric acid (CA) combined with the precursors of dopants. In previous publications on the fabrication of S,N-GQDs, thioure was often used as the source of S and N [19–22]. The use of this material has the advantage of using only one chemical for co-doping S and N but its disadvantage is almost impossible to change the concentration of each doping element. Zheng Binjie et al. used a microwave-assisted hydrothermal technique in which the carbon source is glucose and ammonium is the nitrogen source to prepare N-GQDs [23]. However, fabrication of N, S activated GQDs by microwave-assisted pyrolysis from a mixture of CA, 3-mercaptopropionic acid (MPA), and ammonium to fabricate S,N-GQDs has not been published yet. By using two different precursors, authors hope that it is possible to vary the concentration of each impurity to obtain the desired results for applications. Therefore, in this study, microwave-assisted pyrolysis from CA, MPA, and ammonia precursors, which is a new method with an advantage in the low-cost, rapid and environmentally friendly process, was used to prepare GQDs and S,N-GQDs. The low-cost method originated from cheap precursors and low energy consumption. After that, the structure and optical properties of the prepared GQDs and S,N-GQDs materials were systemically investigated under different fabrication conditions as well as doping elements. The optical characterization of S,N-GQDs was taken out through photoluminescence (PL), absorption (Abs), and quantum yield (QY). Finally, the photoluminescence mechanisms of S,N-GQDs were also discussed in detail.

S,N-GQDs were prepared via microwave-assisted pyrolysis from precursors of CA, ammonia, and MPA, which were carbon (C), N, and S sources. In detail, CA and MPA precursors were proportionally weighed and dissolved in double-distilled water with an amount of 5 ml in a beaker. Then, a 0.02 M ammonium hydroxide solution was supplemented to the solution mixture to achieve a pH of 7. Finally, the solution was placed in the microwave oven for the pyrolysis process with a power of 720 W in 3 min, which was shown in a diagram in Fig. 1. When the solution changed a dark brown color, 50 ml of double-distilled water was added to the solution to obtain the final product. The quantum efficiency and optical properties of S,N-GQDs can be varied by different factors, such as the pH value of the original solution, the ratio of S and/or N source to carbon, and the power and time of the microwave process used for synthesis. So, it is required to optimize experimental conditions to prepare S,N-GQDs with enhanced photoluminescence in comparison with undoped GQDs. For doped GQD materials in this study, besides investigating the effect of dope, we also investigated the influence of microwave power from 400 W to 800 W, as well as the pH of the original solution.

The crystalline phase of the fabricated S,N-GQDs was investigated via an X-ray diffractometer (XRD, Siemens D5005). The quality of S,N-GQDs samples was determined and evaluated by using Raman spectroscopy (HORIBA XploRA micro-Raman). The Fourier Transform Infrared (FTIR) spectra were recorded using FT-IR Spectrum Two Spectrometer Perkin Elmer - US, in the wavenumber ranging from 4000 to 500 cm−1. Photoluminescence (PL) properties were studied by the Fluorolog-322 measurement system (Horiba Jobin-Yvon) using a 450 W Xenon lamp for excitation. The absorption properties of the dispersed samples were studied by a Cary 60 UV-Vis spectrometer (Agilent) in the 200–1100 nm region. The quantum yield (QY) of S,N-GQDs in water was estimated using quinine sulfate in 0.1 M H2SO4 with a QY of 0.54 (as the reference material) [24,25].

The crystalline phase structure of S,N-GQDs was evaluated by XRD patterns, as shown in Fig. 2(a). Both diffraction patterns of GQDs and S,N-GQDs appeared to have a wide peak ranging from 20° to 35°, which corresponds to the lattice plane of (002). The appearance of a wide diffraction peak indicated the nanoscale size of these materials. The peak center of GQDs is located at 23.4°, while that of S,N-GQDs is located at 27.4°. The peak shift to a higher angle of XRD pattern in the S,N-GQDs sample comparison to that of the GQDs sample can be assigned to the presence of functional groups and dopants. This result is similar to the observation in the doped GQDs fabricating by other methods [17,23,26]. Raman spectra are carried out to evaluate the disorder of the structure of samples based on different types of defects, such as edges, defects, stresses, strains, functional groups, number of layers, and many others. Fig. 2(b) presents Raman spectra of GQDs and S,N-GQDs, which indicates two bands at 1353 and 1594 cm–1 correlating with G and D bands, respectively. In which, the G band is attributed to the E vibration modes of the aromatic sp2 carbon domain and the D band is related to defects or structural disorders of the sp2 domain. The D and G bands intensity ratio (ID/IG), which depends on the type of GQDs synthesis method, is used to study the order of structure between the crystal phase and amorphous systems of the graphite domain [27]. The ID/IG ratio of GQDs is 0.9 while one of S,N-GQDs is 1.06. The higher ID/IG ratio of the hetero-atomically doped GQDs indicated that there are many defects on the surface of the S,N-GQDs [27]. To confirm the existence of bonds when doping S and N into the GQDs host, FTIR spectra were recorded in the 4000–500 cm−1 region. Fig. 2(c) shows the FTIR spectra of the GQDs and S,N-GQDs samples. For both fabricated samples, we observe multiple absorption bands at different wavelengths corresponding to different oscillations: bands at around 1451 cm−1 are assigned to C-O oscillations; bands at 1649 cm−1 are attributed to the CC ring oscillations, bands at 1714 cm−1 are due to the ring CO oscillations [20,28]; and a band at 3440 cm−1 which is characteristic for O-H bond [20,28,29]. When co-doping S, N into GQDs, the FTIR spectrum appears new bands at 1361 cm−1 and ∼1699 cm−1 corresponding to CN oscillations; the band at ∼1299 cm−1 is assigned to the N-CO oscillation; the band at 1189 cm−1 belongs to the C-NH-C oscillation [17,30]. Simultaneously, bands at 638 cm−1 and ∼1081 cm−1 corresponding to the C-S and CS oscillations [29,31] are also observed. As can be seen, the FTIR spectrum has verified the presence of S and N elements in the GQDs host lattice. The morphological properties and average sizes of the S,N-GQDs could be determined through TEM image which is shown in Fig. 2(d). The result showed that the pretty uniform spherical particles have an average diameter of 3 nm. This result is similar to previous reports on S,N-GQDs fabricated by other methods [17,19]. For example, Majid Masteri-Farahani et al. synthesized S,N-GQDs with an average size were about 3–4 nm by simple hydrothermal process from citric acid and thiourea [19]. As reported by Ramada et al., where S,N-GQDS was fabricated by the hydrothermal method which showed good dispersion of GQD without agglomeration with size 3–7 nm [17]. Overall, with not much change in structural properties by doping, the synthesis of S,N-GQDs by microwave-assisted pyrolysis has been successful.

Effects of heteroatoms on the optical properties of GQDs, S-GQDs, N-GQDs, and S,N-GQDs synthesized by microwave-assisted pyrolysis were investigated by photoluminescence (PL) spectra as displayed in Fig. 3. For the undoped GQDs sample, the PL band locating at 414 nm is due to the Cπ* → Cπ emission transition of C atoms in the host lattice. By doping, the emission band position shifted to a higher wavelength range, which was 420 and 470 nm for N-GQDs and S-GQDs, respectively. The emission peak of the S-GQDs sample shifts towards a longer wavelength than that of the N-GQDs sample, indicating that the S-doping forms lower energy levels compared to that of the N doping [24,32,33]. By co-doping S and N heteroatoms, the emission peak was located at 448 nm, which was mostly in the middle of the emission peak positions of S-GQDs and N-GQDs. This PL band could be attributed to the simultaneous emission transition of two energy levels of S-GQDs and N-GQDs samples. Besides that, the absorption spectra of the prepared samples with different heteroatoms are also shown in the inset of Fig. 3. The results show that the GQDs sample has only one absorption band at 278 nm. Doping N and S into the GQDs added other absorption peaks at 328 nm and 338 nm, respectively. By co-doping S and N, the absorption spectrum appeared with two peaks at 285 and 338 nm. Thus, when doping N or S or co-doping S and N, besides the Cπ → Cπ* emission transition, there is also a Cπ → Nπ* or Cπ → Sπ* transition, leading to a shift towards the longer wavelength of the emission peak. At the same time, there are absorption peaks due to Cπ → Oπ* electronic transitions from present CO groups [15,16,24,32–34] or the surface states [35].

To study the effect of microwave power as well as initial pH values on the optical characterization of the materials, S,N-GQDs samples were fabricated with different microwave powers and pH values. The emission spectra of the prepared S,N-GQDs samples with microwave power from 400 W to 800 W are shown in Fig. 4, which shows all broad spectra. The PL peak at 448 nm characterized the transitions between the energy levels of the doped ions. The change in PL intensity of the samples was investigated through QY, the calculation results are listed in Table 1. QY of S,N-GQDs increases with increasing microwave power and reached a maximum value of 48.8% for a power of 720 W, leading to a significant increase in luminescence efficiency. When the microwave power reached 800 W, the QY decreased to a value of 46.8%. This reduction could be a result of the removal of hydroxyl groups that contribute to radiative recombination or the graphitic flake formation at higher power [36]. The absorption spectra of S,N-GQDs samples under various microwave powers are also portrayed in the inset of Fig. 4. The absorption spectrum includes two bands, which located at 282 and 336 nm, are attributed to the Cπ → Cπ* and Cπ → Oπ* transitions, respectively. These results are consistent with published works, which suggest that the GQDs sample has absorption peaks that are characterized by π – π∗ transitions of the CC bond [24,33]. Besides that, the absorption band located in the 340–500 nm region is due to the absorption transition of the non-bonding electrons of N(C-N) and S(C-S) [33]. Thus, the PL peak at 448 nm could be an overlap of two peaks at ∼420 nm and ∼470 nm, which result from the electron-hole recombination at C-N/CN and C-S/CS states in N-GQDs and S-GQDs, respectively [33].

PL spectra of the S,N-GQDs samples, which were fabricated at a microwave power of 720 W with pH values of 3, 7, and 12, are shown in Fig. 5. The results show that the emission peaks of the samples shifted from 452 nm to 446 nm as the pH values increased from 3 to 12. This blue shift of emission peak could be attributed to the decrease in surface states. In addition, the luminescence intensity of the S,N-GQDs samples was also modified by changing pH values. The position of the absorption peaks was almost unchanged with the varying pH values (as presented in the inset of Fig. 5). The QY of S,N-GQDs samples were also calculated, and the results were 28.5%, 48.8%, and 31.2% for pH values of 3, 7, and 12, respectively. Overall, the samples prepared with a microwave power of 720 W and a pH value of 7 could show the highest QY due to the complete reaction of precursors.

The absorption and fluorescence mechanism of S,N-GQDs could be explained through the energy diagram given in Fig. 6 . Theoretical research on doping heteroatoms into GQDs has been published by Feng J. et al. [14]. This study showed that when doping heteroatoms, three positions in which the doping elements are substituted include the surface plane (type-I), the edge plane with a hexatomic ring (type-II) and the pentatomic ring (type-III). Accordingly, depending on the position where the heteroatoms are replaced, blue-shift or red-shift of absorption spectra can observe. In the experiment, the effect of heteroatom on the photoluminescence behavior of GQDs relates to the type of dopants, the doping method, concentrations of heteroatom dopants, and the final structure of doped GQDs [1]. For instance, nitrogen doping in GQDs through the photochemical method shown an enhancement of the concentration of pyrrolic nitrogen and a decrease in the number of oxygen-containing groups, which would cause a blue shift in the photoluminescence spectra of GQDs [1,37]. On the other hand, adding nitrogen into the interior of the sp2 domains was reported to shrink the bandgap and cause a redshift in the photoluminescence spectra of GQDs [1]. In our work, when S and N were co-doped into GQDs, there was a redshift for both absorption and emission peaks. The cause of the redshift can be attributed to the substitution of these S and N atoms into the edge plane with a hexatomic ring (type-II). This leads to the formation of energy levels which located between π and π * levels of carbon. Typical, when N and S were co-doped into GQDs, they made energy levels Nπ* and Sπ* which located between π and π * levels of carbon. These observed Nπ* and Sπ* levels can be generated from the defect in the primary structure of GQDs or oxygen, which relate to the functional groups located on the edge of the sheet. In addition, unlike N, the existence of the C–S bond is attributed to the similar electronegativity of S (2.58) and C (2.55) [4]. Moreover, because sulfur is less electronegative than nitrogen, doping sulfur causes the insertion of the Sπ* orbital below the Nπ* orbital [19]. The possible transfer from the electron transitions to photon transitions in S,N-GQDs samples can be taken as follow: under excited by light, the electron transfer from the state Cπ to the excited states Cπ* (Cπ→Cπ*) as arrow (1) and (2), and Cπ → Oπ* as in arrow (3). Then these excited electrons will release through two following mechanisms: Fist mechanism is the direct combination between the same energy levels, which leads to the observed emission in arrow (4). The other is a non-radiative transition through Cπ* →Nπ*, Cπ* →Sπ* or through Oπ* →Nπ*, Oπ* →Sπ*, then the Nπ* → Cπ emission recombination to produces PL (5) and the Sπ* → Cπ recombination to produces PL (6). With the two above mechanisms, the emission spectrum of the S,N-GQDs sample is a broadband as observed in Fig. 3.

S,N-GQDs have been successfully synthesized by microwave-assisted pyrolysis using CA, ammonium, and MPA as precursors. This is a fast and environmentally friendly method with easy-to-find precursors but high luminescence efficiency. The absorption and luminescence peaks of GQDs were at 278 nm and 414 nm, which were related to the Cπ → Cπ* transitions. When S and N co-doped into GQDs, there was a redshift for both absorption and PL peaks, with values of 285 and 335 mm (for the absorption spectrum) and 448 mm (for the PL spectrum). These peaks were related to the Cπ → Sπ* and Cπ → Nπ* transitions, besides the Cπ → Cπ* and Cπ → Oπ* transition. The highest QY could be found in the S,N-GQDs with microwave power and pH values of 720 W and 7, respectively.

Le Xuan Hung conceived and prepared ideas, designed the experiments, carried out characterizations (including the TEM, AFM, XPS, and FTIR), and wrote and revised the manuscript. Trinh Vu Tuyet Nhung designed the experiments and prepared GQDs, S-GQDs, N-GQDs, and S,N-GQDs. Ho Van Tuyen conceived and designed the experiments, prepared and purified the samples, performed the PL characterization, and prepared the draft manuscript. Ngo Tran wrote and revised the manuscript and advised on the N-GQDs’ purification.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research is funded by the Vietnam National Foundation for Science and Technology Development (NAFOSTED) under grant number 103.03-2020.22.
